target.name: Arteminorin C
target.motivation: Arteminorin C is a naturally occurring nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarin with xanthine oxidase (XOD) inhibition activity, isolated from Artemisia minor. Its total synthesis has not yet been reported due to the lack of general approaches for nonsymmetric 3,3'-bicoumarins.

success_criteria.free_text: Total synthesis of Arteminorin C, 3,3'-Biisofraxidin, and Biscopoletin, demonstrating a general approach to nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins.

key_disconnections.reasoning: The authors proposed a two-step Perkin reaction sequence. The first Perkin reaction forms a coumarin acetic acid derivative, and the second Perkin reaction between this coumarin acetic acid and a salicylaldehyde derivative forms the bicoumarin. This strategy avoids transition metal catalysis and allows for the synthesis of nonsymmetrical bicoumarins.

alternatives_considered.strategy: Coupling reagent mediated esterification followed by intramolecular Knoevenagel condensation
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Low yields (13-46%) were observed with various coupling reagents (Mukaiyama, CDI, EDCI, PPh3/I2), attributed to decreased nucleophilic reactivity of the salicylaldehyde OH group.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy is based on two consecutive Perkin reactions. The first Perkin reaction forms a coumarin acetic acid from a salicylaldehyde derivative and succinic anhydride. The second Perkin reaction then couples this coumarin acetic acid with a different functionalized salicylaldehyde to form the nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarin. This modular approach allows for the synthesis of various nonsymmetrical bicoumarins and is metal-free.

thought.background: Existing methods for 3,3'-bicoumarin synthesis are mainly for symmetrical compounds, often relying on transition metal-promoted oxidative dimerization. Only one example of nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarin synthesis exists, using Pd-catalyzed decarboxylative coupling, but it is limited to two examples.
thought.gap: There is a need for novel and general strategies to access nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins in a simple and efficient way.
thought.decision: To address the lack of general approaches for nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins, we propose a strategy employing two consecutive Perkin reactions. The first Perkin reaction will form a coumarin acetic acid from a salicylaldehyde derivative, and a second Perkin condensation between this coumarin acetic acid and a new functionalized salicylaldehyde will form the nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins.

evidence.text_span: Herein, we report a synthetic approach for the construction of nonsymmetrical 3,3′-bicoumarins 9 through a Perkin reaction between substituted 2-(2-oxo-2H-chromium-3-yl)- acetic acids 7, also prepared by a Perkin condensation, and substituted salicylaldehydes 8 (Scheme 1b) without the use of an external solvent and under metal-free conditions.
evidence.section: Introduction

thought.background: The first step of the proposed strategy is the synthesis of functionalized 2-(2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acids (7) via a Perkin reaction.
thought.gap: Optimizing the yield of these coumarin acetic acid intermediates.
thought.decision: We began our study by preparing a series of functionalized 2-(2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acids 7 through a Perkin reaction following the conditions reported by Zhou and coworkers in 2013. We will test both conventional heating (Method A) and microwave irradiation (Method B) to improve yields.

outcome.observation: Compounds 7a-f were obtained in low to moderate yields using Method A (conventional heating). Method B (microwave irradiation in DMF) generally increased yields, ranging from 36-97%. Lower yields for 7d and 7e were observed due to gummy solid formation.

evidence.text_span: We began our study by preparing a series of functionalized 2-(2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acids 7 through a Perkin reaction following the conditions reported by Zhou and coworkers in 2013.14 Then, by employing various substituted salicylaldehydes (1.0 equiv), succinic anhydride (3.2 equiv), and triethylamine (2.6 equiv) under solvent-free conditions and conventional heating (method A), compounds 7a−f were obtained in low to moderate yields (Scheme 2). To improve the yield of coumarin acetic acid 7, a modification of the reaction conditions was tested. Accordingly, the use of dimethylformamide (DMF) as the solvent and heating under microwave irradiation using the same stoichiometric ratio (method B) resulted in an increased yield in most cases, giving the corresponding 2-(2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acids 7 in 36−97% yields. Using method B, substrates 7i and 7j, the precursors of biscopoletin, 3,3′-biisofraxidin, and arteminorin C, were also prepared in good yields. The lower yield observed for compounds 7d and 7e using method B might be due to the formation of a gummy solid that hindered the proper stirring of the reaction mixture and prevented complete consumption of the starting material.
evidence.section: Synthesis of Functionalized 2-(2-Oxo-2Hchromen-3-yl)acetic Acids 7

thought.background: With the coumarin acetic acid (7c) prepared, the next step is to form the 3,3'-bicoumarin (9a) by reacting it with a salicylaldehyde derivative (8a). One proposed approach involves using a coupling reagent to form an ester intermediate (11), which would then cyclize.
thought.gap: Identifying suitable coupling reagents and conditions for this approach.
thought.decision: We started the synthesis of 3,3′-bicoumarins using 2-(7- methoxy-2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acid (7c) and 3,5-ditert-butyl-2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (8a) as model substrates with different coupling agents. First, the Mukaiyama reagent was used in the presence of DIPEA in DCM at 40 °C.

outcome.observation: 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a was obtained in a modest 15% yield.

evidence.text_span: We started the synthesis of 3,3′-bicoumarins using 2-(7- methoxy-2-oxo-2H-chromen-3-yl)acetic acid (7c) and 3,5-ditert-butyl-2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (8a) as model substrates with different coupling agents (Table 1). Thus, when the Mukaiyama reagent was used in the presence of DIPEA in DCM at 40 °C, 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a was obtained although in a modest 15% yield (Table 1, entry 1).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The previous attempt with Mukaiyama reagent in DCM gave low yield. We need to improve the reaction conditions.
thought.gap: Optimizing solvent and temperature for the Mukaiyama reagent approach.
thought.decision: The expected compound was obtained with almost the same chemical yield by changing the solvent to 1,2-dichloroethane (1,2-DCE) and increasing the temperature to 80 °C under microwave irradiation.

outcome.observation: Yield remained low (14%) despite changing solvent and increasing temperature.

evidence.text_span: The expected compound was obtained with almost the same chemical yield by changing the solvent to 1,2-dichloro-ethane (1,2-DCE) and increasing the temperature to 80 °C under microwave irradiation (Table 1, entry 2).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The Mukaiyama reagent with DIPEA gave low yields. A stronger base might improve the reaction.
thought.gap: Investigating the effect of a stronger base on the Mukaiyama reagent approach.
thought.decision: When a stronger base such as DBU was used, 9a was obtained in 46% yield after the reaction mixture was heated at 80 °C under microwave irradiation.

outcome.observation: Yield improved to 46%, but still not satisfactory.

evidence.text_span: When a stronger base such as DBU was used, 9a was obtained in 46% yield after the reaction mixture was heated at 80 °C under microwave irradiation (Table 1, entry 3).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: Other coupling reagents might be more effective.
thought.gap: Exploring alternative coupling reagents for the bicoumarin formation.
thought.decision: Subsequently, by changing the coupling reagent to carbonyldiimidazole (CDI) and using DIPEA in acetonitrile (MeCN) at 60 °C, only traces of compound 9a were obtained.

outcome.observation: Only traces of product 9a were obtained.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, by changing the coupling reagent to carbonyldiimidazole (CDI) and using DIPEA in acetonitrile (MeCN) at 60 °C, only traces of compound 9a were obtained (Table 1, entry 4).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: CDI was ineffective. Another coupling reagent, EDCI, should be tested.
thought.gap: Evaluating EDCI as a coupling reagent.
thought.decision: Afterward, N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDCI) was tested in the presence of DMAP, affording 9a in 18% yield.

outcome.observation: Product 9a was obtained in 18% yield, which is still low.

evidence.text_span: Afterward, N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide (EDCI) was tested in the presence of DMAP, affording 9a in 18% yield (Table 1, entry 5).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: Previous coupling reagents yielded low or trace amounts of product. We should try conditions reported in the literature for similar reactions.
thought.gap: Testing literature-reported conditions for 3-arylcoumarin synthesis.
thought.decision: Next, we proceeded to test the reaction conditions reported by Phakhodee,15 but we isolated only 13% of 9a.

outcome.observation: Only 13% of 9a was isolated, indicating these conditions are not effective for our system.

evidence.text_span: Next, we proceeded to test the reaction conditions reported by Phakhodee,15 but we isolated only 13% of 9a (Table 1, entry 6).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The PPh3/I2 system gave low yield. We should try a different halogen source.
thought.gap: Investigating NBS as a replacement for molecular iodine in the PPh3-mediated reaction.
thought.decision: On the other hand, when NBS replaced molecular iodine, the reaction did not proceed.

outcome.observation: The reaction did not proceed when NBS was used.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, when NBS replaced molecular iodine, the reaction did not proceed (Table 1, entry 7).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: All attempts using coupling reagents for the second coumarin unit formation resulted in low yields or no reaction. This was attributed to the decreased nucleophilic reactivity of the salicylaldehyde OH group.
thought.gap: Finding a more efficient method for the construction of the second bicoumarin unit.
thought.decision: With this in mind, we then proceeded to evaluate a Perkin reaction for the building of the second coumarin unit to target 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a. Employing the same model substrates 7c and 8a, a Perkin reaction was tested using acetic anhydride and TEA as the base. After heating at 80 °C under microwave irradiation for 1 h, 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a was obtained in 61% yield.

outcome.observation: The Perkin reaction provided 9a in 61% yield, a significant improvement over previous attempts.

evidence.text_span: We speculated that the low yield obtained for the construction of the second bicoumarin unit was attributed to the decreased nucleophilic reactivity of the OH group in salicylaldehyde due to the presence of the carbonyl electron-withdrawing group at the ortho position, making the esterification reaction inefficient. With this in mind, we then proceeded to evaluate a Perkin reaction for the building of the second coumarin unit to target 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a. Employing the same model substrates 7c and 8a, a Perkin reaction was tested using acetic anhydride and TEA as the base. After heating at 80 °C under microwave irradiation for 1 h, 3,3′-bicoumarin 9a was obtained in 61% yield (Table 1, entry 8).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The Perkin reaction showed promise with a 61% yield. We need to further optimize the reaction conditions.
thought.gap: Investigating the effect of reaction time on the Perkin reaction.
thought.decision: The increase in the reaction time to 2 h was not beneficial for the reaction outcome.

outcome.observation: Increasing reaction time to 2 h decreased the yield to 51%.

evidence.text_span: The increase in the reaction time to 2 h was not beneficial for the reaction outcome (Table 1, entry 9).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: Longer reaction time was detrimental. We should try increasing the temperature.
thought.gap: Investigating the effect of higher temperature on the Perkin reaction.
thought.decision: On the other hand, an increase in reaction temperature to 120 °C afforded product 9a in a slightly better 62% yield.

outcome.observation: Increasing temperature to 100 °C resulted in a 48% yield, which is lower than the 80 °C result.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, an increase in reaction temperature to 120 °C afforded product 9a in a slightly better 62% yield (Table 1, entry 10).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The Perkin reaction at 80 °C for 1h gave 61% yield. Increasing temperature to 100 °C gave 48%. The text mentions 120 °C gave 62% yield, which is the best so far.
thought.gap: Finalizing the optimal conditions for the Perkin reaction.
thought.decision: After evaluating both approaches, we determined that the best reaction conditions were as follows: 1.2 equiv of acid 7c, 1.0 equiv of salicylaldehyde derivative 8a, 3 equiv of triethylamine, and 18.0 equiv of acetic anhydride at 120 °C under microwave irradiation for 1 h.

outcome.observation: Optimal conditions for the Perkin reaction were established, yielding 62% of 9a.

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, an increase in reaction temperature to 120 °C afforded product 9a in a slightly better 62% yield (Table 1, entry 11). After evaluating both approaches, we determined that the best reaction conditions were as follows: 1.2 equiv of acid 7c, 1.0 equiv of salicylaldehyde derivative 8a, 3 equiv of triethylamine, and 18.0 equiv of acetic anhydride at 120 °C under microwave irradiation for 1 h (Table 1, entry 11).
evidence.section: Table 1. Optimization of the Reaction Conditions

thought.background: The optimal conditions for the second Perkin reaction have been established. Now, the scope of this approach needs to be evaluated.
thought.gap: Synthesizing a series of nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins by varying the salicylaldehyde derivative.
thought.decision: First, we evaluated the effect of the substituent at salicylaldehyde derivative 8 using 2-(7-methoxy-2-oxo-2Hchromen-3-yl)acetic acid 7c. Under the standard conditions, nonsymmetrical 3,3′-bicoumarins 9b-g were obtained in moderate to good yields.

outcome.observation: Nonsymmetrical 3,3′-bicoumarins 9b-g were obtained in moderate to good yields. Electron-withdrawing groups (e.g., nitro) gave better yields (9e, 73%), while electron-donating groups (e.g., methyl) resulted in moderate yields (9d, 30%).

evidence.text_span: Having established the strategy for the construction of 3,3′- bicoumarins through two consecutive Perkin reactions, we proceeded to evaluate the scope of this approach (Scheme 4). First, we evaluated the effect of the substituent at salicylaldehyde derivative 8 using 2-(7-methoxy-2-oxo-2Hchromen-3-yl)acetic acid 7c. Under the standard conditions, nonsymmetrical 3,3′-bicoumarins 9b-g were obtained in moderate to good yields. We observed a remarkable electronic effect of the substituent on the yield. For instance, the highly electron-withdrawing nitro group at salicylaldehyde derivative 8 gave the best results, generating product 9e in 73% yield. In contrast, the presence of an electron-donating group, such as the methyl group, resulted in a moderate 30% yield (9d).
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Synthesis of 3,3′-Bicoumarins

thought.background: The effect of salicylaldehyde substituents has been studied. Now, the effect of different coumarin acetic acids needs to be explored.
thought.gap: Synthesizing a series of nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins by varying the coumarin acetic acid.
thought.decision: Next, we explored the use of different coumarin acetic acids 7 in combination with 3,5-di-tert-butyl-2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (8a). In general, good yields were obtained for the corresponding 3,3′-bicoumarins 9h-j.

outcome.observation: Good yields were obtained for 3,3′-bicoumarins 9h-j.

evidence.text_span: Next, we explored the use of different coumarin acetic acids 7 in combination with 3,5-di-tert-butyl-2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (8a). In general, good yields were obtained for the corresponding 3,3′-bicoumarins 9h-j.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Synthesis of 3,3′-Bicoumarins

thought.background: The modular nature of the approach allows for combining different coumarin acetic acids and salicylaldehydes.
thought.gap: Synthesizing a diverse set of nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins using various combinations of starting materials.
thought.decision: Subsequently, we explored a modular approach by the combination of different coumarin acetic acids 7 and functionalized salicylaldehydes 8 to obtain a series of structurally diverse nonsymmetrical 3,3′-bicoumarins. Some functional groups such as methoxy, methyl, tert-butyl, nitro, and chloro were well tolerated, and products 9l-p, respectively, were obtained in yields ranging from 48% to 86%.

outcome.observation: Products 9l-p were obtained in yields ranging from 48% to 86%, demonstrating good tolerance for various functional groups.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we explored a modular approach by the combination of different coumarin acetic acids 7 and functionalized salicylaldehydes 8 to obtain a series of structurally diverse nonsymmetrical 3,3′- bicoumarins. Some functional groups such as methoxy, methyl, tert-butyl, nitro, and chloro were well tolerated, and products 9l-p, respectively, were obtained in yields ranging from 48% to 86%.
evidence.section: Scheme 4. Synthesis of 3,3′-Bicoumarins

thought.background: The general strategy for nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins has been established. Now, it needs to be applied to the total synthesis of natural products, including Arteminorin C.
thought.gap: Synthesizing the protected intermediate 9q for Biscopoletin.
thought.decision: To this end, 3,3′-bicoumarin 9q was prepared from 7i and 8i under standard conditions.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 9q was obtained in 35% yield.

evidence.text_span: We then demonstrated the synthetic utility of the approach with the total synthesis of arteminorin C, 3,3′-biisofraxidin, and biscopoletin (Scheme 5). To this end, 3,3′-bicoumarin 9q was prepared from 7i and 8i. On the other hand, using coumarin acetic acid 7j, compounds 9r and 9s were obtained under the standard conditions using salicylaldehydes 8j and 8k, respectively (for the preparation of salicylaldehydes, see the Supporting Information).
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Total Synthesis of Biscopoletin, 3,3′-Biisofraxidin, and Arteminorin C

thought.background: Intermediate 9q, a protected form of Biscopoletin, has been synthesized.
thought.gap: Deprotecting 9q to obtain Biscopoletin.
thought.decision: Finally, catalytic hydrogenolysis of 9q-s with Pd/C and H2 gave biscopoletin, 3,3′-biisofraxidin, and arteminorin C, respectively.

outcome.observation: Biscopoletin was obtained in 95% yield.

evidence.text_span: Finally, catalytic hydrogenolysis of 9q-s with Pd/C and H2 gave biscopoletin, 3,3′-biisofraxidin, and arteminorin C, respectively.
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Total Synthesis of Biscopoletin, 3,3′-Biisofraxidin, and Arteminorin C

evidence.text_span: On the other hand, using coumarin acetic acid 7j, compounds 9r and 9s were obtained under the standard conditions using salicylaldehydes 8j and 8k, respectively (for the preparation of salicylaldehydes, see the Supporting Information).
evidence.section: Scheme 5. Total Synthesis of Biscopoletin, 3,3′-Biisofraxidin, and Arteminorin C

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for Arteminorin C is the product of yields from step 2 (for 7j, 61%), step 21 (for 9s, 49%), and step 22 (for Arteminorin C, 99%). This calculates to approximately 29.6%.

validation.significance: The developed strategy provides a novel and general metal-free approach for the synthesis of nonsymmetrical 3,3'-bicoumarins through iterative Perkin reactions. This method was successfully applied to the total synthesis of natural products arteminorin C, 3,3'-biisofraxidin, and biscopoletin, which were previously difficult to access. The synthesized compounds also exhibit interesting luminescent properties, suggesting further applications.

source.title: Synthesis of Nonsymmetrical 3,3'-Bicoumarins: Total Synthesis of Arteminorin C, 3,3'-Biisofraxidin, and Biscopoletin.
source.authors: Castañón-García M, López-Mendoza P, Galicia-Badillo D, Miranda LD.
